title: Kawarabayashi–Rossman Conjecture
statement_text: There exists a universal constant $c$ such that every graph with pathwidth $\Omega(k^c)$ has treewidth at least $k$ or contains a subdivision of a complete binary tree of height $k$.
context_text: Kawarabayashi and Rossman stated this conjecture in the context of studying treedepth and pathwidth (SODA 2018). The paper's Theorem 1.1 implies the conjecture with $c = 2$, which the authors show is best possible.
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture with $c = 2$ via Theorem 1.1: every graph with treewidth at most $t-1$ has pathwidth at most $th+1$ or contains a subdivision of a complete binary tree of height $h+1$.
paper_title: Approximating pathwidth for graphs of small treewidth

title: Wood's Conjecture on Pathwidth and Binary Trees
statement_text: Every graph with treewidth $t$ and not containing a subdivision of a complete binary tree of height $h$ has pathwidth at most $f(t) \cdot h$ for some function $f$.
context_text: Wood conjectured a bound of the form $f(t) \cdot h$ on the pathwidth for graphs of treewidth $t$ that avoid a subdivision of a complete binary tree of height $h$. The paper notes that Theorem 1.1 implies this conjecture.
paper_contribution: Theorem 1.1 establishes the conjecture with $f(t) = t$, showing pathwidth at most $th+1$ for graphs of treewidth at most $t-1$ that avoid a subdivision of a complete binary tree of height $h+1$.
notes: Exact original statement not reproduced in the paper; described only as 'a bound of the form f(t) · h on the pathwidth for some function f'. Also referenced at [20, Conjecture 6.4.1].
paper_title: Approximating pathwidth for graphs of small treewidth

title: Dean's Problem on FPT Pathwidth Parameterized by Treewidth
statement_text: Is pathwidth fixed-parameter tractable when parameterized by treewidth? That is, does there exist a computable function $f$ and an algorithm that computes $\mathrm{pw}(G)$ for an $n$-vertex graph $G$ in $f(\mathrm{tw}(G)) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ time?
context_text: First raised by Dean, this long-standing open problem asks whether pathwidth can be computed exactly in FPT time parameterized by treewidth. The known XP algorithm of Bodlaender and Kloks runs in $n^{f(\mathrm{tw}(G))}$ time, which is not FPT.
paper_contribution: Partially addresses the problem by providing the first polynomial-time approximation algorithm for pathwidth whose ratio depends only on treewidth, achieving an $O(\mathrm{tw}(G)\sqrt{\log \mathrm{tw}(G)})$-approximation; described as a step toward the exact FPT question.
paper_title: Approximating pathwidth for graphs of small treewidth

title: Fomin–Thilikos Open Problem on Approximating Pathwidth Parameterized by Treewidth
statement_text: Is there a polynomial-time algorithm that approximates the pathwidth of an $n$-vertex graph $G$ to within a factor $g(\mathrm{tw}(G))$ for some function $g$, with running time polynomial in $n$?
context_text: Fomin and Thilikos observed that even obtaining any approximation of pathwidth when parameterized by treewidth (with polynomial running time in $n$) was open, complementing the exact FPT question of Dean.
paper_contribution: Fully resolves the problem: gives a polynomial-time $O(\mathrm{tw}(G)\sqrt{\log \mathrm{tw}(G)})$-approximation algorithm for pathwidth, with running time polynomial in the graph size.
notes: Problem is stated informally in the introduction without a labelled environment; paper describes its algorithm as 'a solution to the latter problem (in a strong sense)'.
paper_title: Approximating pathwidth for graphs of small treewidth
